Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered because I don't have internet at home, so I had to plan everything around when I could get to the library or use someone's hotspot. I'd usually try to download as much as possible during those windows and then study from my notes and printed materials later. I aimed for about 13 hours a week, but it was never at the same times—more like grabbing whatever moments I could between my extracurricular activities and helping out at home.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on when I could actually access the internet and what deadlines were coming up. Some weeks I'd cram everything into two or three intense sessions at the library, other weeks I'd spread it out more if I had better access. Deadlines pushed me to be more strategic about when I logged in and what I prioritized downloading first.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was new content posted or when assignments were due soon. Since getting online is such a production for me, I tried to make each login count—checking announcements, downloading videos and readings, posting in discussions if I had time. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important that week.
Q4: Yeah, it became routine in the sense that every time I got to the library, the LMS was my first stop. I'd developed this whole system—check announcements, download videos, save PDFs, post any discussion responses I'd written offline. It wasn't daily like some students, but it was definitely a consistent pattern whenever I had connectivity.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful because I could download them and watch multiple times offline while taking notes. I also found the discussion forums helpful because they made me think through concepts and see other perspectives. The reading materials in PDF format were great too since I could print them or save them to study without internet.
Q6: I tended to skip the interactive quizzes and anything that required constant internet connection because I just couldn't access them reliably. Some of the supplementary readings I'd delay because I had to prioritize the core materials first. It wasn't that I didn't want to use them—I just had to be practical about what I could actually access given my situation.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand the material better or if it counted toward my grade. I also valued things I could take offline with me, like downloadable content or discussion prompts I could think about and respond to later. If something required being online the whole time with no offline option, it felt less accessible to me.
Q8: Required activities always got priority because I couldn't afford to miss them, but optional ones felt like a luxury I couldn't always access. When I did have extra time online, I'd try to explore optional materials, but honestly most of the time I was just trying to keep up with what was mandatory. It's not that optional stuff wasn't interesting—I just had to be strategic.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded files and handwritten notes. I'd print readings at the library, watch downloaded videos on my phone without internet, and use textbooks I borrowed. A huge chunk of my 13 study hours happened completely disconnected from the LMS because that's just my reality.
Q10: So many times! I'd download a video once and then watch it three or four times while taking detailed notes—but the LMS only saw one view. Or I'd print a PDF and spend hours annotating it and making flashcards, but there's no record of that. My best studying often happened at home with no internet, working through problems in my notebook.
Q11: All the time—that was basically my main strategy. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then study them for days offline. One video might show as watched once, but I'd actually review it multiple times and pause to take notes. The LMS data really doesn't capture how much I engaged with content after that initial download.
Q12: There were definitely times when I felt really bored and disconnected, especially when I couldn't participate in real-time discussions or when I'd fall behind because of access issues. Sometimes the stress of juggling everything—extracurriculars, home responsibilities, getting to the library—just made it hard to feel motivated. Those weeks my attendance and assignment completion definitely suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from everything else going on, I'd use my limited online time less efficiently—just going through the motions instead of really engaging. Confusion about assignments was hard because I couldn't just quickly ask questions or look things up; I had to wait until my next library visit. Competing responsibilities meant sometimes the LMS got the bare minimum from me.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was just elsewhere—tired or stressed or bored. I'd have the video playing but not really absorbing anything, or I'd click through readings without processing them. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I was trying to at least show up even when I wasn't fully present.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important, partly because getting online to submit them was its own challenge. Sometimes I'd have the work done but couldn't submit it until I got to the library, and by then I'd lost momentum. Other times I was just bored or overwhelmed and avoided things that felt too difficult to tackle with my limited resources.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated when I made the effort to get online. I'd plan my library visits around due dates and prioritize whatever was most urgent. Without a deadline pushing me, I might let things slide because getting online required so much effort. Deadlines were stressful but also the main thing keeping me on track.
Q17: When I got feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar content or try harder on the next assignment. But sometimes feedback came when I couldn't easily access the LMS, so there'd be a delay before I could even see it and adjust. Positive feedback especially helped because it reminded me my effort was paying off despite my challenges.
Q18: The course structure helped when materials were released in advance so I could plan my downloads, but it hurt when things were time-sensitive or required synchronous participation. I appreciated when modules were self-contained so I could work through them offline at my own pace. The flexibility was crucial for someone in my situation.
Q19: Downloadable content made engagement so much easier for me—anything I could take offline was gold. But features requiring constant connectivity or real-time participation were basically inaccessible. Discussion forums were good because I could read posts, think about them offline, and compose responses to post later. More offline-friendly design would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying—logging in, watching videos, reading materials—but struggling to actually complete and submit work. Maybe they're confused, or facing obstacles outside the course, or just not connecting with the material despite their efforts. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's more about trying to figure things out.
Q21: That's basically me! My LMS activity looks lower than my actual engagement because so much of my studying happens offline. I managed a Merit grade with decent exam scores because I was genuinely learning, just not in ways the system could track. Students like me might be doing tons of work that's invisible to the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when students access materials and how often, which gives some indication of engagement timing. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement, and the real obstacles students face. They can't tell if someone watched a video five times offline or spent hours with printed notes—they just see that one download.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections, and trying to apply what I'm learning—whether I'm online or not. It's about mental investment, not just clicking through content or showing up in the data.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I study offline with downloaded materials and printed notes—probably more than half my total study time. It doesn't capture the resilience it takes to keep going despite not having internet at home, or how I've had to develop creative strategies just to participate. My engagement is way deeper than my login frequency suggests.
